text:
The separation of propylene from propane through adsorption is important and highly efficient process that can potentially replace the expensive energy demanding cryogenic distillation. Although molecular sieving materials generally show higher selectivity, the adsorption capacity of the materials is generally low. So far, the high performance adsorbents that could raise the adsorption capacity while keeping high selectivity are rare. In this work, we report for the first time an oxyfluoride-based ultramicroporous metal-organic framework, **UTSA-400**, denoted as [Ni(WO2F4)(pyz)2], (pyz = pyrazine). This 1D porous material exhibits a unique selectivity for propylene over propane, meanwhile showing a remarkable capacity for propylene adsorption.

The active sites over the pores of UTSA-400 material are represented by the exposed oxide/fluoride pairs leading to significant improvement of host-guest interaction and a corresponding increase in the propylene uptake. Propane is specifically excluded by controlled accessibility of the material and high selectivity toward propylene adsorption is established. Upon investigation of mechanism by using in situ characterization techniques such as infrared spectroscopy, and dispersion corrected density functional theory (DFT-D), the as-synthesized material affords column breakthrough experiments towards the direct production of polymer grade propylene (>99.5%) at high dynamic productivity. The rational design and atomic precision tailoring of pores in MOFs materials offers routes to improve significantly the guest species binding within the material eliminating any fundamental limitations related to selectivity vs capacity in adsorptive separations.
